title: On the Description of Conductivity in PVA-Based Composite Polymer Electrolytes: EMT Approach
abstract:
phys. stat. sol. (a) 172, 329 (1999)
Subject classification: 72.80.Le; S12
On the Description of Conductivity
in PVA-Based Composite Polymer Electrolytes:
EMT Approach
P. K. Shukla and S. L. Agrawal1
Department of Physics, A.P.S. University, Rewa (MP) 486 003, India
(Received June 11, 1998; in revised form October 19, 1998)
In the present work, the attempt has been made to explain the influence of filler concentration on
the conductivity response of PVA-based composite polymer electrolytes following the Effective
Medium Theory (EMT) approach. In the present investigation, the variation in the thickness
of the highly amorphous space charge layer (responsible for enhancement in conductivity),
covering the dispersed grains, has been taken into account while interpreting the conductivity
data. During the estimation of the surface layer conductivity the role of glass transition tem-
perature and degree of crystallinity were also considered. The simulated conductivity data incor-
porating these parameters in the EMT model show a better fit to the experimental conductivity
response.
1. Introduction
Solid polymer electrolytes have recently received considerable attention over the inor-
ganic solid electrolytes mainly due to their potential applicability in various electroche-
mical devices working at ambient temperatures [1 to 5]. Amongst various macromole-
cules, polyethylene oxide (PEO) is one of the most extensively studied polymer
matrices which shows semicrystalline nature under ambient conditions and forms crys-
talline complexes with most of the salts used as ionic dopants. However, it has been
recognised that in such electrolytes ionic transport occurs in the amorphous region and
is governed by the segmental motions of polymer chains [6]. Therefore, to inhibit crys-
tallisation various approaches have been proposed and a number of flexible amorphous
macromolecules possessing sufficient ability to complex cation have been studied in the
past two decades [1,7,8]. Ambient temperature conductivities as high as 10±±4 S cm±±1
have been reported for few modified systems. Unfortunately, most of these highly
amorphous systems possess the problem of mechanical integrity as well as electroche-
mical stability and electrode corrosion especially in the presence of added plastici-
zers.
As an alternative approach, the concept of composite polymer electrolytes (CPEs)
has been introduced and widely explored by several research groups in recent years [1,
9 to 15]. It has been found that, besides the enhancement in electrical conductivity
(except in few cases) dispersal of inorganic/organic fillers also improves the mechanical
as well as electrochemical stability of the polymer matrix and extends its temperature
stability range [14 to 18].
1 Corresponding author; Fax: +9107662-42175; e-mail: spa@apsrewa.mp.nic.in
P. K. Shukla and S. L. Agrawal: Conductivity in Composite Polymer Electrolytes
329
 In spite of the extensive research on composite polymer electrolytes, only a few studies
attempt to explain the mechanism of ionic conductivity in these electrolytes. This is due to
the fact that addition of a third component further complicates its structure. Recently, ap-
plication of the Effective Medium Theory (EMT) approach has shown to be a good tool to
understand the conductivity response of CPEs [12,19,20]. However, to the best of my
knowledge, such a work on composite polymer electrolytes has been mainly confined to
polyether based systems. It should be stressed here that the interfacial region (the layer
covering dispersed filler grains) as well as size of dispersed grains play a major role in
describing the overall conductivity of the composite system through the EMTapproach. In
order to explain the conductivity response of PEO based CPEs dispersed with amorphous
organic fillers, Przyluski et al. [20] have suggested the inclusion of variations in glass transi-
tion temperature and volume fraction of the interface layer in their EMT calculations.
In view of the above, in the present work an attempt has been made to apply effec-
tive medium theory approach alongwith these considerations to describe conductivity of
a new composite polymer electrolyte system, viz. (PVAx : NH4SCN)y : PVAc. Further,
the effect of the variation in volume fraction of the amorphous shell (interface layer)
has also been included in an attempt to improve the goodness of the fit. A comparison
between experimental room-temperature conductivity data and that calculated on the
basis of EMT approach is presented.
2. Experimental
In the present work composite polymer electrolytes were synthesised using the well-
known solution cast technique. Weighed amounts of the starting materials Polyvinyl
Alcohol (PVA) (Rolex make; MW 78,000), Ammonium Thiocynate (NH4SCN) (SdFine
Chem. make; AR grade) and Polyvinyl Acetate (PVAc) (Aldrich Chem. make; MW
237,000) for varying stoichiometric proportions were separately dissolved in a common
solvent Dimethyl Sulfoxide (DMSO) (SdFine Chem. make; spectroscopy grade) and
subsequently mixed together. The mixture was rigorously stirred to form viscous homo-
genous solution. The final solution was then poured in Teflon moulds and placed in a
dry-box to allow normal solvent evaporation. The final retention of solvent and other
volatile
contaminants
was
carried
out
by
vacuum
drying
of
these
samples
at
10±±4 to 10±±5 Torr. The (PVAx : NH4SCN)y : PVAc composite films of different thick-
ness (0.005 to 0.05 cm) were casted for each composition x and y, where x, y represent
the weight fractions of NH4SCN and PVAc, respectively, in the composite system. In
the present investigation samples were prepared for three different x-values; namely
0.05, 0.10 and 0.15 while y varied in the range from zero to 0.30 for each of the x-
values. All the synthesised samples were kept in vacuum desiccator to avoid any atmo-
spheric contamination. Prior to each measurement samples were repeatedly dried under
vacuum for more than 12 hours to avoid the effect of humidity. The relative humidity
was maintained at 30% during all the measurements. Complex impedance data were
recorded with the help of an LCR meter (Hioki make; model 3520) to evaluate the
ionic conductivity of as-synthesised samples. For the measurement thermally evapo-
rated aluminum electrodes (thickness 800 to 1000 A) were used in the cell assembly
Al/CPE/Al. Thermal analyses of CPEs were performed on a DuPont (series 2000) ther-
mal analyser for the quantitative description of morphology. The DSC data were subse-
quently used to estimate s1 and t=R parameters required for EMT calculations.

P. K. Shukla and S. L. Agrawal: Conductivity in Composite Polymer Electrolytes
In spite of the extensive research on composite polymer electrolytes, only a few studies
attempt to explain the mechanism of ionic conductivity in these electrolytes. This is due to
the fact that addition of a third component further complicates its structure. Recently, ap-
plication of the Effective Medium Theory (EMT) approach has shown to be a good tool to
understand the conductivity response of CPEs [12,19,20]. However, to the best of my
knowledge, such a work on composite polymer electrolytes has been mainly confined to
polyether based systems. It should be stressed here that the interfacial region (the layer
covering dispersed filler grains) as well as size of dispersed grains play a major role in
describing the overall conductivity of the composite system through the EMTapproach. In
order to explain the conductivity response of PEO based CPEs dispersed with amorphous
organic fillers, Przyluski et al. [20] have suggested the inclusion of variations in glass transi-
tion temperature and volume fraction of the interface layer in their EMT calculations.
In view of the above, in the present work an attempt has been made to apply effec-
tive medium theory approach alongwith these considerations to describe conductivity of
a new composite polymer electrolyte system, viz. (PVAx : NH4SCN)y : PVAc. Further,
the effect of the variation in volume fraction of the amorphous shell (interface layer)
has also been included in an attempt to improve the goodness of the fit. A comparison
between experimental room-temperature conductivity data and that calculated on the
basis of EMT approach is presented.
In the present work composite polymer electrolytes were synthesised using the well-
known solution cast technique. Weighed amounts of the starting materials Polyvinyl
Alcohol (PVA) (Rolex make; MW 78,000), Ammonium Thiocynate (NH4SCN) (SdFine
Chem. make; AR grade) and Polyvinyl Acetate (PVAc) (Aldrich Chem. make; MW
237,000) for varying stoichiometric proportions were separately dissolved in a common
solvent Dimethyl Sulfoxide (DMSO) (SdFine Chem. make; spectroscopy grade) and
subsequently mixed together. The mixture was rigorously stirred to form viscous homo-
genous solution. The final solution was then poured in Teflon moulds and placed in a
dry-box to allow normal solvent evaporation. The final retention of solvent and other
10±±4 to 10±±5 Torr. The (PVAx : NH4SCN)y : PVAc composite films of different thick-
ness (0.005 to 0.05 cm) were casted for each composition x and y, where x, y represent
the weight fractions of NH4SCN and PVAc, respectively, in the composite system. In
the present investigation samples were prepared for three different x-values; namely
0.05, 0.10 and 0.15 while y varied in the range from zero to 0.30 for each of the x-
values. All the synthesised samples were kept in vacuum desiccator to avoid any atmo-
spheric contamination. Prior to each measurement samples were repeatedly dried under
vacuum for more than 12 hours to avoid the effect of humidity. The relative humidity
was maintained at 30% during all the measurements. Complex impedance data were
recorded with the help of an LCR meter (Hioki make; model 3520) to evaluate the
ionic conductivity of as-synthesised samples. For the measurement thermally evapo-
rated aluminum electrodes (thickness 800 to 1000 A) were used in the cell assembly
Al/CPE/Al. Thermal analyses of CPEs were performed on a DuPont (series 2000) ther-
mal analyser for the quantitative description of morphology. The DSC data were subse-
quently used to estimate s1 and t=R parameters required for EMT calculations.
P. K. Shukla and S. L. Agrawal
3. Theoretical Considerations. EMT Model
The approach is based on a phenomenological model by Nan and Smith [21,22] which has
been recently extended for application to composite polymer electrolytes [12,19,20,23].
For composite polymer electrolytes it has been recognised that changes in polymer micro-
structure occur at the interface between matrix electrolyte and the dispersed filler
[11,19] and the enhancement in conductivity is connected with the existence of a highly
conductive layer at the polymer filler interface. Therefore, in the composite system
there are three components (Fig. 1) with different electrical properties, namely (1) a
highly conductive layer covering the surface of a grain or particle additive, (2) dis-
persed insulating grains or particles and (3) a polymer matrix ionic conductor. We can
consider a dispersed grain covered with the thin interface layer as a unit and call it a
composite grain (combination of components 1 and 2). According to the Maxwell-
Garnett rule [24] the equivalent conductivity sc of the composite unit can be ex-
Here s1 and s2 are, respectively, the conductivity of interface layer and that of dis-
persed insulating grain. Y is the volume fraction of dispersed grain in a composite unit
and for spherical geometry can be calculated as
where t is the thickness of interface layer and R the radius of dispersed grain.
According to the above consideration, the composite electrolyte can be treated as a
quasi two-phase mixture consisting of an ionically conductive polymer matrix and com-
posite units. The electrical properties of such a quasi two-phase mixture can simply be
described by the Bruggeman equation [25] or the simple effective medium equation
introduced by Landauer [26]. These simple approaches ignore the local field effects and
are suitable only for the description of quasi two-phase mixtures for volume fractions
of composite units lower than 0.1. In the quasi two-phase mixture as the volume frac-
tion of composite units increases, the composite grains gradually join together to form
complicated clusters and then the local field effects cannot be ignored. Nan [21,22] and
Nakamura [27] have considered the following limiting situations to incorporate this fact:
Description of Conductivity in PVA-Based Composite Polymer Electrolytes
Fig. 1. Schematic representation of composite polymer
electrolyte: 1, 2, 3 depict the three phases present in CPE
(i) The matrix phase is the ionic conductor (here polymer electrolyte) and isolated
composite units are scattered throughout (for very low concentration of dispersed phase).
(ii) The matrix phase consists of overlapping or touching composite units with a small
amount of electrolyte in the interstices (for very high concentration of dispersed phase).
Because the local field effect is a geometrical effect and has no relation with the
conductivity of the two phases, and since the matrix phase screens the effect of dis-
persed phase in the limiting composite unit [21], the equivalent conductivity bounds, sa
c of the two limiting cases mentioned above, can be calculated according to a
simplified form of Nakamura's equations [22,27]:

c  scd ÿ 1 vc=d ÿ vc  2scvc=3 ÿ vc ;
where vc  v2=Y, is the volume fraction of composite units, v2 is the volume fraction of
dispersed grains in the bulk electrolyte, s3 is the conductivity of matrix polymeric elec-
trolyte, and d is the dimensionality of the system which is equal to three for spherical
grains considered in the present description. After introducing the improved conductiv-
ity parameters (eqn. (3)) in the self-consistent EMT equation suggested by Kirpatrick
[28], the conductivity sm of the composite electrolyte can be expressed as
where pc is the continuous percolation threshold for the composite grains. Since the com-
posite grains are allowed to overlap in the region of interface layer, pc can be taken to be
0.28 for this random mixture on the basis of general percolation theory [22,29].
According to eqn. (4) one arrives at the highest enhancement in ionic conductivity
when the composite units fill the total volume of the electrolyte, i.e., when vc  1. This
occurs for a characteristic value of v2 equal to v*2 given by
For concentrations of filler exceeding v*2, the quasi two-phase system consists of a mix-
ture of composite grains and dispersed bare insulating grains and no matrix electrolyte
other than that present in the composite units. For such a situation, eqn. (4) can be
rewritten in the following form:
2 (the modified conductivity parameters for the second limiting case)
are calculated according to the method proposed by Nan [22] and Nakamura [27] and
c  2sc1 ÿ v2  v*2=21  v2 ÿ v*2 ;
2  2s2v2 ÿ v*2=3 ÿ v2  v*2 :
Similar to eqns. (3a) and (3b), these equations (7a) and (7b) also consider local field
effects. Here Pc is the percolation threshold of the dispersed bare insulating grains
which are not allowed to overlap. Therefore, Pc in eqn. (6) is different from pc in
P. K. Shukla and S. L. Agrawal
eqn. (4) and has been taken as the percolation threshold for a general random mixture,
i.e. 0.15 [21,22]. Equations (4) and (6) enable us to calculate the conductivity sm of
composite polymer electrolytes.
The applicability of the model presented above has been tested by Wieczorek and
co-workers [11,19,20,23,30] for PEO-based composite polymer electrolytes with dis-
persed inorganic (stiff) and organic (soft) fillers. They found that for volume fraction of
dispersed phase greater than v*2, the conductivity drops more rapidly than predicted by
the model presented above. It has been shown that the t=R ratio decreases for concen-
trations of filler exceeding v*2, i.e. the concentration for which the maximum of conduc-
tivity is reached. Moreover, to improve the goodness of the fit, it was assumed that the
conductivity of the amorphous shell changes with the change of filler concentration.
With the help of DSC data it was shown that Tg of the composite electrolyte varies
with filler concentration. Since the structure of the interface layer is highly amorphous,
the temperature dependence of conductivity would follow the VTF relation
s  ATÿ0:5 exp ÿEa=kT ÿ T0
and the pseudo activation energy Ea and the pre-exponential factor A in this equation
would be independent of the filler concentration. Therefore, the interface layer conduc-
tivity is only dependent on T0 and hence on Tg. The Tg values taken from DSC experi-
ments were used for calculation of s1 for the electrolytes with different concentrations
of the filler. To generalise the variation in Tg on the basis of the work of Bares [31]
and Schneider and diMarzio [32], they have proposed following empirical equation for
the Tg of immiscible blends and mixed phase system:
Tg  K0  K1fv2=S2  K2fCs; v2 ;
where v2 and S2 are respectively the volume fraction and surface area of the filler, Cs
is the molar concentration of the dopant salt in the complex, and K0, K1, K2 are
adjustable parameters. It has been found that for a system with different concentrations
of filler additive and a fixed concentration of dopant salt, the best fit to the experimen-
tal data can be obtained when Tg is approximated by the relation
In this equation, the effect of salt is included in K0 which is identical to the Tg for
pristine polymer electrolyte. K1 describes the influence of filler added on Tg of the
composite system and K2 is connected with polymer±fillersalt interactions.
On the other hand, estimation of the t=R parameter is based on the assumption that
an increase in amorphous phase content for the composite electrolyte in comparison to
the pristine system is due to the amorphous phase present in the surface layer (compo-
nent 1 in Fig. 1). Therefore, on the basis of eqn. (2), the volume fraction of this compo-
nent and hence the t=R parameter can be approximated as [19]
where v2 is the volume fraction of filler (PVAc) and v1 the volume fraction of the sur-
Description of Conductivity in PVA-Based Composite Polymer Electrolytes
Inclusion of the variation of Tg and t=R parameters with filler concentration in the
model presented above for the calculation of the composite electrolyte conductivity has
been found to fit well with the experimental results for a series of polyether based
composite polymer electrolytes.
(PVAx : NH4SCN)y : PVAc composite polymer electrolytes using eqns. (4) and (6) and
later incorporate the assumption of variations of s1 and t=R as an extension of the
theory to improve the goodness of the fit with the experimental results. Though, all the
three sets of CPEs (Section 2) were investigated, the analysis hereafter concerns with
reference to the CPEs for x = 0.15 and 0 < y  0:30.
As said earlier, the model connects the enhancement of conductivity with the existence
of a highly amorphous space charge layer at the polymer±filler interface. Therefore, the
relative enhancement in conductivity is mainly related to the conductivity of this space
charge layer which in turn is dependent on Xc and Tg of the matrix polymer in the
composite electrolyte system concerned. Maximum enhancement in conductivity is gen-
erally observed for the filler concentration where a minimum in degree of crystallinity
(Xc) and Tg is attained. The critical concentration (v*2) (where the composite units fill
the total volume), often corresponds to this filler concentration (i.e. filler concentration
where v2  v*2  Y). The variation of Xc data for the system concerned shows a mini-
mum for v2  0:064 and hence was taken as v*2 for the present EMT calculations. The
other three parameters s1, s2 and s3 defined in the model were assigned to the values
7.9  10±±4, 5  10±±14, and 7  10±±5 S cm±±1, respectively. The conductivity of the shell
(s1) was assumed as the ambient temperature conductivity of the fully amorphous pris-
tine complex and extracted from the extrapolation of the experimentally obtained con-
ductivity data in the high-temperature regime [33]. The conductivity of the filler PVAc
(s2) was taken from the work of Wintersgill et al. [34], while s3 corresponds to the
room-temperature conductivity of the pristine complex (PVAx : NH4SCN) for x = 0.15.
Further, the percolation threshold values were chosen as pc = 0.28 and Pc = 0.15 on the
basis of the work of Siekierski [12]. The conductivity sm of the CPEs calculated from
EMT equations (eqns. (4) and (6)) is depicted in Fig. 2 alongwith the experimental
data. The simulated conductivity values from this model roughly show an order of mag-
nitude difference with the experimental data for filler concentration v2 < v*2 while it is
small for v2 > v*2. The observed differences between experimental and theoretically si-
mulated conductivity response are probably on account of the considerations about
fixed s1 as well as t=R ratio in this model [19,20]. In the case of composite polymer
electrolytes, both these parameters should be considered as variables on account of the
(i) The change in conductivity of the surface layer (s1) is on account of variation in
Tg and Xc values of the composite polymer electrolyte with change of filler concentra-
(ii) The thickness of the surface layer decreases with increase of filler concentration
up to the critical concentration v2  v*2.
(iii) Beyond the critical concentration, the radius (R) of the bare insulating grains in-
creases due to the formation of filler agglomerates leading to lowering of the t=R ratio.
P. K. Shukla and S. L. Agrawal
Though Przyluski and co-workers [19,20] have pointed these facts, they have not in-
corporated variations in t=R with v2 into their EMT calculations to explain the conduc-
tivity behaviour of PEO-based composite polymer electrolytes. The goodness of fit to
the experimental data for several systems obtained by these workers on considering
only the variations in s1 prompted us to do the same for the present system. In view of
the above, relevant conductivity parameters were obtained for the present system and
listed in Table 1. It should be noted that the so-called quasi-equilibrium glass transition
temperature (T0) was evaluated from the relation T0 = Tg ±± 60 K, empirically found to
be the best fit for the present system. Thermal expansion coefficient data were used to
establish this correlation. The constants K0, K1 and K2, in eqn. (10) were obtained by
non-linear least-square regression analysis on the experimentally found Tg data. The
best-fit Tg data were subsequently used to evaluate the surface layer conductivity (s1).
The variation of these parameters, namely Tg and s1, with volume fraction of PVAc in
The EMT calculations including the variation in surface layer conductivity were per-
formed for different t=R ratios, where the volume fraction of the surface layer attains
an optimum (Table 2). The best qualitative fit of eqns. (4) and (6) to the experimental
conductivity data was obtained for t=R ratio 1.06. The so obtained theoretical plot is
depicted in Fig. 2 (dashed line). It is observed from this figure that the minimum in
conductivity at very low volume fraction of the filler (v2  0:018 corresponds to the
first percolation threshold for the conductivity (where vc  pc). Further, it is observed
that for filler concentration v2 < 0:16, the conductivity variation remains almost similar
to that for the simple Nan and Smith model (Fig. 2, solid line) except that the conduc-
tivity values are slightly lowered on account of variation in s1 governed by the varia-
tion in Tg of the CPE. Beyond this concentration (v2 > 0:16), the conductivity variation
Description of Conductivity in PVA-Based Composite Polymer Electrolytes
Fig. 2. Isothermal conductivity of CPEs (for x = 0.15) as a function of volume fraction of the filler.
Solid line: Nan and Smith model, dashed line: model including correction for variations in s1, tri-
angles: model including corrections for variation in s1 and t=R, circles: experimental data
exhibits a significant dip on account of the rapid increase in Tg of the CPE (Fig. 3). The
comparison of simulated data for the two cases (i.e. simple Nan and Smith model and
the model after incorporating variation in s1) reveals a better fit in the latter case.
In wake of factors (ii) and (iii) outlined above the difference between theoretical and
experimental data can be further scaled down by incorporating t=R variations alongwith
s1 variation in the present EMT calculations. A continuous decrease in t=R ratio with

LTR: low-temperature region; HTR: high-temperature region
b) The VTF equation s  ATÿ0:5 exp ÿEa=kT ÿ T0**

VTF relation. Tg of surface layer has been calculated according to eqn. (10) using the
parameters K0 = 345.4 K, K1 = 509.7 K, K2 = 2122.5 K
Fig. 3. Tg; exp (squares), Tg; theor (solid line) and log s1 (dashed line) as a function of volume fraction
of the filler in CPEs (for x = 0.15)
filler concentration has also been reported in recent past for CPEs dispersed with or-
ganic fillers [19,20]. However, to the best of our knowledge t=R variations have not
been included in EMT calculations. Consequently, to improve the goodness of the fit,
here we have also included t=R variation alongwith variation in s1 values over the
entire concentration range of filler as a next step in the EMT calculations. The t=R
ratio and other relevant parameters for different filler concentrations have been calcu-
lated from the DSC data (Table 2). In accordance with eqn. (11), the t=R ratio is found
to decrease beyond v2  0:064, owing to insignificant variation in the volume fraction
v1 over a fairly large filler concentration range in the proximity of v*2. However, for still
higher concentrations the observed decrease in v1 causes a marked change in the t=R
ratio. The conductivity response obtained after inclusion of these parameters (s1 and
t=R) is depicted in Fig. 2 (triangles). This curve displays a better fit to the experimental
data in comparison to the previous calculations (Fig. 2, solid and dashed lines) espe-
cially beyond v2  v*2. The impact of the t=R parameter for v2  v*2 is not apparent in
EMT calculations because the composite units remain isolated in this situation.
Interestingly, the maximum in conductivity is correctly predicted by the present EMT
calculations and lies in the range 0.06 to 0.12 of volume fraction of the filler which
corresponds to v2  v*2 where vc  1. Further, it is clear that the morphology of compo-
site polymer electrolytes highly influences the conductivity behaviour since variation in
s1 and t=R ratio with concentration of soft organic filler are related to morphological
changes occurring in CPE. Thus, their inclusion in EMT calculations is obvious to fit
the theory with experiments. Despite the inclusion of morphological effects a large dif-
ference (around one order of magnitude) between experimental and theoretical results,
especially in the low concentration regime, is observed and hence further modifications
are required to make the theory a vital tool for the analysis of conductivity in compo-
Description of Conductivity in PVA-Based Composite Polymer Electrolytes
Estimated values of the t=R ratio and other related parameters for different filler con-

the percentage degree of crystallinity of crystalline part of PVA in the CPE;

to the volume fraction of PVA in the CPE

(PVAx:NH4SCN) for x  0:15 and estimated by multiplying (1 ±± XpPVA) to
the volume fraction of PVA in the pristine electrolyte
volume fraction of the surface layer (component 1 in Fig. 1) and calculated as

site polymer electrolytes. In our opinion considerations regarding the shell geometry
need attention for better estimation of the two conductivity bounds sa
(eqns. (3a) and (3b)). This would further lead to a better treatment of the t=R param-
eter in the theory. Fractal approach can be adopted for a better description of shell
geometry in the theory and thereby improving the goodness of the fit particularly when
applied to composite polymer electrolytes.
In the present work the conductivity response of PVA-based composite polymer elec-
trolytes has been studied using the EMT approach. The effect of morphological changes
in the shell conductivity s1 and t=R ratio has also been included in the present EMT
calculations. The so obtained simulated conductivity response shows a better fit with
the experimentally observed variations in conductivity with filler concentration espe-
cially for v2  v*2. The maximum in conductivity is well predicted by the present EMT
calculations and lies in the range of 0.06 to 0.12 volume fraction of the filler concentra-
tion which corresponds to v2  v*2; the critical concentration where vc  1. Deviations
from the goodness of the fit outline the necessity of further modifications in the theory,
especially in the considerations regarding shell geometry, when applied to composite
